perturbation.original_text: The Adviser shall create and maintain all necessary records in accordance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, including, but not limited to, records required by Section 31(a) of the Act and the rules thereunder, as the same may be amended from time to time, pertaining to the various functions performed by it and not otherwise created and maintained by another party pursuant to contract with the Trust.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser shall create and maintain records as it deems necessary, irrespective of Section 31(a) of the Act and the rules thereunder, as amended, pertaining to the functions performed, even if another party maintains similar records under contract with the Trust.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the Adviser can maintain records as it deems necessary, irrespective of Section 31(a), it creates a conflict, as this section specifies required records. Also, the original text states that if other parties keep the records, the Adviser does not need to do so. This was changed to say that even if another party keeps the same records, the Adviser will still keep them.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, which mandates specific record-keeping requirements for investment advisers. The changed text suggests the adviser has discretion over record-keeping, conflicting with the legal requirement to maintain records as prescribed by the Act and related rules.

Accounts and records (a) Maintenance of records (1) In general Each registered investment company, and each underwriter, broker, dealer, or investment adviser that is a majority-owned subsidiary of such a company, shall maintain and preserve such records (as defined in section 78c(a)(37) of this title ) for such period or periods as the Commission, by rules and regulations, may prescribe as necessary or
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940. The original text requires the Adviser to maintain records in accordance with applicable laws, including Section 31(a). The changed text states the Adviser can maintain records 'as it deems necessary, irrespective of Section 31(a),' which negates the legal requirement to maintain specific records as mandated by the Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 80a-30, states that investment advisers 'shall maintain and preserve such records...for such period or periods as the Commission, by rules and regulations, may prescribe as necessary.' The changed text's allowance for discretionary record-keeping conflicts with this mandatory requirement, making the contract non-compliant and creating a legal contradiction under Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: The Adviser may rely on information reasonably believed by it to be accurate and reliable. Except as may otherwise be required by the Act or the rules thereunder, neither the Adviser nor its directors, officers, employees, shareholders, agents, control persons or affiliates of any thereof (collectively, the "Adviser Employees") shall be subject to any liability for, or any damages, expenses or losses incurred by the Trust in connection with any error of judgment, mistake of law, any act or omission in connection with or arising out of any services rendered under or payments made pursuant to this Agreement or any other matter to which this Agreement relates, except by reason of willful misfeasance, bad faith or gross negligence on the part of any such persons in the performance of the duties of the Adviser under this Agreement or by reason of reckless disregard by any of such persons of the obligations and duties of the Adviser under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser is not liable for any damages or losses incurred by the Trust, irrespective of errors of judgment, mistakes of law, acts, or omissions, even in cases of gross negligence, as long as the Adviser acted in good faith.
perturbation.explanation: This modification drastically limits the Adviser's liability, contradicting the original clause and general fiduciary duties. The original text allowed for liability in the case of willful misfeasance, bad faith, or gross negligence. The modified text removes the gross negligence clause, even though the Adviser is still expected to act in good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Investment Advisers Act of 1940 - Fiduciary Duty
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the fiduciary duty imposed on investment advisers under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940. While good faith is important, it doesn't absolve the adviser of liability for gross negligence, which is a breach of fiduciary duty. The Act requires advisers to act with reasonable care and diligence, which this modification undermines.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 275.206(4)-8 - Pooled investment vehicles. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 275.206(4)-8 - Pooled investment vehicles. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 275.206(4)-8 Pooled investment vehicles. (a) Prohibition. It shall constitute a fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative act, practice, or course of business within the meaning of section 206(4) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-6(4) ) for any investment adviser to a pooled investment vehicle to: (1) Make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, to any investor or prospective investor in the pooled investment vehicle; or (2) Otherwise engage in any act, practice, or course of business that is fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative with respect to any investor or prospective investor in the pooled investment vehicle. (b) Definition. For purposes of this section “pooled investment vehicle” means any investment company as defined in section 3(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-3(a) ) or any company that would be an investment company under section 3(a) of that Act but for the exclusion provided from that definition by either section 3(c)(1) or section 3(c)(7) of that Act ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-3(c)(1) or (7)). [ 72 FR 44761 , Aug. 9, 2007] Investment Company Act of 1940 CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the fiduciary duty imposed on investment advisers, specifically regarding gross negligence. The original text held the adviser liable for willful misfeasance, bad faith, or gross negligence. The changed text removes the gross negligence clause, stating the adviser is not liable even in cases of gross negligence if acting in good faith. The Law Explanation states that while good faith is important, it doesn't absolve the adviser of liability for gross negligence, which is a breach of fiduciary duty. 15 USC 80b-6 prohibits investment advisers from engaging in any practice that defrauds clients. By removing liability for gross negligence, the modified contract allows for actions that could harm the client, even if done in good faith, which contradicts the adviser's duty to act with reasonable care.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended at any time by written instrument executed by each of the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement can be altered or changed by oral agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification contradicts the Statute of Frauds. Most jurisdictions require certain contracts, especially those involving significant financial transactions or lasting more than a year, to be in writing to be enforceable. Removing the "written instrument" clause allows for oral modification, which is generally unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing oral amendments, the modified text violates the Statute of Frauds as codified in Massachusetts law (M.G.L. c. 259 § 1), which requires certain agreements, particularly those not to be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. This is intended to prevent fraudulent claims and misunderstandings.
perturbation.justification: The modified text, which allows for oral agreements, directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds. The Statute of Frauds generally requires certain contracts, especially those not to be performed within one year, to be in writing to be enforceable. By removing the requirement for a "written instrument," the changed text creates a situation where oral modifications, which may be unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, are permitted. Because this falls under Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, and based on the Perturbation and Law Explanations, the changed text contradicts the original legal intent.
